part: A Grandmother Who Wanted Her People to Do Something
title: The Slave Mart in Fayetteville
summary: Interviewed by David Cline in High Point, North Carolina, on October 1, 2013, Robert Brown opens with the grandmother who raised him, Nelly Marshall Brown of Anson County, and her father Joseph Kimmel Sr., a slave used to build North Carolina's railroads, noting that he himself was later appointed by Governor Jim Martin to head the North Carolina Railroad Company.

title: This Was the Last Place
topic: The Trip to the Slave Mart
summary: Brown recounts his grandmother taking him and his brother as small boys by bus through Fayetteville to stand at the old slave mart, the last place her father was sold, and visits to her run-down clapboard childhood home in Wadesboro with its old Rosenwald school, a trip that gave him an insatiable appetite for the history of slavery and Reconstruction.

title: White Water and Colored Water
summary: Born in High Point in February 1935 into poverty and segregation, Brown remembers his favorite Woolworth store and the day his grandmother caught him drinking from the white fountain, telling him it was all God's water and that one day God would fix it, a day she lived to see.

title: My First Major Client
summary: Brown describes how in 1961 his first major client became the F.W. Woolworth Company, which he urged to get ahead of the coming change, advising its board of major financiers like John J. McCloy and Fred Kirby on integrating the company, then being sent by them to counsel other CEOs across America.

title: An Escort for Mary McLeod Bethune
summary: Returning to his schooling, Brown recalls Leonard Street School and William Penn High School, founded by the Quakers, and being chosen by the principal to escort visitor Mary McLeod Bethune around the school for hours as she counseled him, alongside other returning speakers like Langston Hughes.

title: Mrs. Loudon's Newspapers
summary: Brown tells how, too poor to buy books, he scrounged magazines and newspapers and became editor of his school paper, and how a neighbor, Mrs. Loudon, who worked for a wealthy white family, began bringing him boxes of discarded New York newspapers and magazines two or three times a month, feeding a reading habit he keeps to this day.

title: You Can Put the Dots Together
summary: Brown explains that he still reads many newspapers daily from different parts of the country so that when he sits on corporate boards or makes presentations in New York, London, or Paris, he speaks from fact rather than theory, able to connect events across places because he built the knowledge base to do it.

title: Coming Around Again
summary: Brown argues that race-related problems are returning to American companies because current management has no frame of reference for them, the people who lived through the earlier phase having died, retired, or gone, leaving firms unprepared to deal with issues that recur in full circle every thirty or forty years.

title: The Highest Mark on the Exam
summary: Brown recounts a year at Virginia Union, returning home to North Carolina A&T when his grandparents fell ill, taking a police exam tied to a sociology course, and the visit from Captain Johnson of the detectives offering the alarmed twenty-one-year-old a job after he scored highest on the examination, which he took for a couple of years.

title: You Are Under Arrest
summary: Brown describes the unwritten rule that black officers were not to arrest white people, and the day he chased a fleeing white man into a branch where he grew up, subdued him with his blackjack, and dragged him out to sign a warrant, an unprecedented arrest around 1956 that, to his surprise, no one challenged.

title: On the Washington Street Beat
summary: Brown tells of arresting a drunken white man who cursed him in a black area while other black residents watched, and how his police work led to a job as a federal agent in New York, where he worked organized-crime cases and testified in the Vito Genovese mafia case before deciding after three years to leave.

part: B and C Associates and the Movement
title: Going Back Where the Action Was
summary: Brown recalls his supervisor warning that he could not run a public relations business in North Carolina, and his answer that he was going home to be in the arena where the sit-ins signaled coming change, founding his firm at the end of 1960 and naming it B and C Associates after a friend named Campbell who soon left.

title: I Underwrote Them
summary: Brown explains how he came to work with Dr. King and his executive vice president Wyatt Tee Walker of the SCLC, using his corporate access to pay hotel and meeting bills the movement could not cover and charging the cost off to major clients like Woolworth as business development.

title: Just Do the Right Thing
summary: Brown describes selling reluctant executives on change for which there was no Harvard Business School equation, drawing on his grandmother's counsel to just do the right thing and to keep helping Dr. King, while making speeches on race relations and crisis handling to company heads at the Westchester Country Club and beyond.

title: The Power of the Black Dollar
summary: Brown tells how he hired A&T economist Sidney Evans and others to document the black consumer market, church, and schools as latent power, pressing Woolworth to add black secretaries, managers, board members, and buyers, and to back black-owned store ownership through sale-leaseback deals across the country.

title: The Big Daddy of Retail
summary: Brown recounts the resistance he met in both black and white communities, leveraging Woolworth's standing in the National Retail Association to push Birmingham stores toward integration, and being sent by Wyatt Tee Walker to test the Peabody Hotel in Memphis, where he became the first black man to take a suite.

title: A Brilliant Tactician
summary: Brown praises Wyatt Tee Walker as a brilliant, under-credited tactician of the movement, then turns to the perilous year of 1968, when he was working with Dr. King on the Poor People's Campaign before King was killed and he went with the King family to Memphis to bring back the body.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

title: Bring Us Together
summary: Brown describes the night before King's funeral hosting figures like Sidney Poitier and Robert F. Kennedy, working briefly on Kennedy's campaign with Earl Graves until Kennedy too was killed, then being recruited through Clarence Townes and Bob Finch onto Nixon's whistle-stop train across Ohio, where a girl's sign reading bring us together became the campaign theme.

title: We Need Your Help
summary: Brown gives his first impression of Nixon as smart, intuitive, and not the racist others tagged, and tells how he first met Dr. King through journalist Stanley Scott and Wyatt Tee Walker over lunch in Atlanta, King telling him plainly that the movement needed the best help it could get.

title: Bring Bob On In
summary: Brown recounts resisting a White House job until Haldeman summoned him to Nixon's transition suite at the Pierre Hotel, where Nixon interrupted a meeting with Herbert Brownell and William Rogers to introduce him as a future principal assistant whom everyone would be seeing a lot of.

title: You Have Total Access
summary: Brown relays Nixon telling him the story of E. Frederic Morrow, the brilliant black aide left sitting by the door without responsibility, and pledging that Brown would instead have total access, which Brown says was never overruled in his four years and three months, even when an association that had funded the campaign tried to get him fired and the President backed him.

title: You Handle This
summary: Brown describes Nixon at the Pierre Hotel handing him the charge to run the black capitalism and minority business effort he had spoken of in the campaign, and promising him a seat at every cabinet meeting he could reach, a standing he held as one of only a handful of senior staff present.

title: This Is Not the Christian Way
summary: Brown tells how, after earlier trips taking black college presidents to South Africa, Coretta Scott King asked his help getting a visa she had been refused, and how he appealed to ambassador Piet Koornhof as a Christian brother over lunch to secure visas and raise money for her delegation around 1986.

title: She Would Not Go Against Them
summary: Brown recounts the delegation meeting Allan Boesak and other South African leaders who, over an all-night discussion, urged Coretta Scott King not to meet President Botha to ask for Mandela's release, and her decision to write Botha a letter of regret instead, which Brown was chosen to deliver to the State House.

title: Send Our Children to School
summary: Brown describes flying with the delegation to Soweto to see Winnie Mandela, who said the thing she and Nelson wanted most was for their children to be able to study in America, a promise others had made and never kept, and how Brown personally paid for seven years of schooling at Boston University for the Mandela grandchildren.

title: You Have Permission
summary: Brown tells how Winnie Mandela petitioned President Botha for him to visit Nelson Mandela, whom no one had seen in over twenty years, and how a call from Niel van Heerden granted permission to see him at Pollsmoor Prison, where the chief warden met Brown warmly and led him to a room to talk as long as he wished.

title: The Memo From the Gate
summary: Brown recounts talking with Mandela for about two hours about what it would take to make peace, writing a memo from scratch notes as soon as he left, and passing it to Winnie Mandela and the ANC, to George Shultz, and to President Reagan through Piet Koornhof, after which real conversations with Mandela began.

title: Half a Box of Books
summary: Brown describes visiting black townships and finding schools with almost no libraries, so he shipped boxes labeled shoes and clothes that were half-filled with school books, moving them free on South African highways, and over the years setting up more than three hundred library areas including in Zululand with the Zulu king.

title: A Devout Quaker, Not a Racist
summary: Brown defends Nixon as a devout Quaker who, whatever language he used off camera, was not a racist, and who genuinely wanted blacks, Hispanics, and others to get a shot in business, making the growth of minority enterprise Brown's charge to carry out.

title: It Did Not Happen by Osmosis
summary: Brown recounts organizing a national commission on minority enterprise chaired by GM's James Roche, who brought automakers to give dealerships to blacks where only two or three had owned them, and crediting such concrete work for Nixon winning more than twenty percent of the black vote in 1972, a share no Republican has matched since.

title: The Pictures No Paper Printed
summary: Brown lists the figures the administration brought in, James Farmer Jr. of CORE as an assistant secretary of HEW and Arthur Fletcher of the Philadelphia Plan among them, the first major federal money for black colleges, and his complaint that not one major newspaper printed the photographs of these meetings.

title: Thirteen Million to a Hundred Forty-Two
summary: Brown describes revamping the EEOC under chairman William H. Brown III, working with Commerce Secretary Maurice Stans through the new Office of Minority Business Enterprise, and pushing every department to award contracts and grants to black firms and colleges, raising federal purchases from black businesses from thirteen million to a hundred forty-two million dollars.

title: Send It Right On Back
summary: Brown tells how, with few black flag officers when Nixon took office, he and General Daniel Chappie James pressed the issue, and how the President agreed to have Brown review the monthly flag-officer list and send it back whenever no minorities appeared, a tactic that opened a path to many black generals before Brown left in 1973.

title: Landing the Plane Unannounced
summary: Brown describes setting up a special commission to investigate race-relations problems on military bases around the world and visiting them himself unannounced, including Hawaii, where the head of CINCPAC was Admiral John S. McCain Jr., father of John McCain, with whom he became friends.

title: They Won't Even Meet With Me
summary: Brown recounts Dr. King summoning him to Cleveland over Sealtest dairy, which refused to meet King about hiring black truck drivers, and Brown flying to New York to tell the CEO of A&P, the largest supermarket chain, that the trouble could reach A&P's stores across many states.

title: We Had Bob Brown on Our Side
summary: Brown tells how the A&P chief ordered Sealtest told that its products would be pulled from every store in Ohio if it did not settle with King, after which Sealtest called King repeatedly begging to meet, and King praised Brown by name at a mass rally at Odie Hoover's Cleveland church.

title: It Did Not Happen Like That
summary: Brown insists that change came not just from marches but from pressure and arm-twisting behind the scenes over jobs, contracts, and opportunity, noting that milk companies once employed thousands of drivers and not one black, and warning that black colleges face closures as student loan money tightens.

title: The Most Proud Spot in the Capital
summary: Brown closes by thanking the interviewers and praising the new National Museum of African American History and Culture as a guidepost for ages yet unborn, recalling that tears well up when he passes its proud spot in Washington, and pledging to bring young people from Africa, where he still sends books, to see it.
